Example 270 | Profile: lower_education_level | Language: English | ID/Index: 270
Source Text
Infection ranges from asymptomatic (in 5 to 50 % of cases) to a hemorrhagic fever with a case fatality rate of up to 50 %.
Generated Simplification
An infection can cause different levels of illness. - Some people may not feel sick at all. This happens in about 5% to 50% of cases. - In other cases, the infection can lead to a serious illness called hemorrhagic fever. This is a condition where bleeding can happen inside the body, and it can be life-threatening. - In the most severe cases, up to 50% of people with hemorrhagic fever may not survive. If you have concerns about symptoms or risks, talk to your doctor.
Reference Simplification
Some infected people do not have symptoms. Others have mild symptoms, and some have a severe, life -threatening illness. Up to 50 % of people with severe bleeding and fever die.

Unlike other dementias, multiple- infarct dementia tends to progress in discrete steps; each episode is accompanied by intellectual decline, sometimes followed by modest recovery.

Unlike other types of dementia, multiple-infarct dementia usually gets worse in steps instead of gradually over time. Reference Simplification
Unlike dementia caused by Alzheimer disease, vascular dementia may progress in steps. Symptoms may worsen suddenly, then plateau or lessen somewhat. They then become worse months or years later when another stroke occurs.

Example 273 | Profile: lower_education_level | Language: English | ID/Index: 273
Source Text
Intractable apneic spells require ventilator support.
Generated Simplification
Some breathing problems, called "intractable apneic spells," can be very serious. These happen when a person stops breathing and cannot start again on their own. In these cases, a machine called a ventilator is needed to help the person breathe. If this happens, it is important to get medical care right away.
Reference Simplification
Newborns who have apnea spells that are difficult to treat may need a ventilator (a machine that helps air get in and out of the lungs) to help them breathe.

Example 274 | Profile: lower_education_level | Language: English | ID/Index: 274
Source Text
Diagnosis of DiGeorge syndrome is based on clinical findings. An absolute lymphocyte count is done, followed by B- and T- cell counts and evaluation of lymphocyte subsets if leukopenia is detected; blood tests to evaluate T- cell and parathyroid function are done. Ig levels and vaccine titers are measured. If complete DiGeorge syndrome is suspected, the T- cell receptor excision circle (TREC) test should also be done. A lateral chest x-ray may help evaluate thymic shadow. Fluorescent in situ hybridization (FISH) testing can detect the chromosomal deletion in the 22q11 region; standard chromosomal tests to check for other abnormalities may also be done.
Generated Simplification
Doctors diagnose DiGeorge syndrome by looking at symptoms and doing specific tests. Here’s how they check for it: - **Blood Tests**: - A test called an "absolute lymphocyte count" is done to measure certain white blood cells. - If the white blood cell count is low, more tests are done to check the types of immune cells (called B-cells and T-cells) and their function. - They also test how well the parathyroid gland is working. This gland helps control calcium levels in the body. - **Immune System Tests**: - Doctors measure immunoglobulin (Ig) levels. These are proteins that help fight infections. - They also check how well vaccines have worked in the body by measuring vaccine titers (a way to see if your body has made protective antibodies). - **Special Tests for Severe Cases**: - If doctors think it might be a more serious form of DiGeorge syndrome, they may do a TREC test. This test looks for signs of how well T-cells are being made. - **Imaging Test**: - A chest x-ray might be done to look for the thymus gland. This gland is important for the immune system, and its absence or small size can be a clue. - **Genetic Testing**: - A test called FISH (fluorescent in situ hybridization) can find a missing piece of chromosome 22, which is often the cause of DiGeorge syndrome. - Other genetic tests may also be done to check for additional problems with chromosomes. If you have questions about these tests or the diagnosis, talk to your doctor. They can explain what the results mean and what steps to take next.
Reference Simplification
Sometimes imaging tests (such as chest x-rays and echocardiography). Doctors suspect DiGeorge syndrome based on symptoms. Blood tests are done for the following reasons: To determine the total number of blood cells and the number of T and B cells; To evaluate how well T cells and the parathyroid gland are functioning; To determine how well the body produces immunoglobulins in response to vaccines. A chest x-ray may be taken to check the size of the thymus gland.
BERTScore
Precision
-0.092
Recall
0.227
F1
0.064
Checklist Evaluation
Item Question Score Reason

C2 2 Background explanations are provided for terms like 'parathyroid gland,' 'immunoglobulin,' and 'vaccine titers.'

C4 1 No examples or comparisons are used, but they are not strictly necessary for understanding.

S3 2 Different ideas and tests are clearly separated into distinct sections.

P2 2 Possible options and consequences of the tests are made explicit.
P3 2 Warnings and next steps are clearly stated, such as discussing results with a doctor.
P4 2 The text advises the patient to discuss questions and results with their doctor.

Example 275 | Profile: lower_education_level | Language: English | ID/Index: 275
Source Text
It had a cranial capacity of 400-500 cc. In 2006, Lee Berger announced the Taung Child probably was killed by an eagle or other large predatory bird since the similarity of damage to the skull and eye sockets of the Taung Child is of that seen in modern primates that are known to have been killed by eagles.
Generated Simplification
The Taung Child had a brain size of about 400-500 cubic centimeters (cc). In 2006, a researcher named Lee Berger shared an important discovery. He explained that the Taung Child was likely killed by an eagle or a similar large bird. This idea came from studying the damage to the skull and eye sockets of the Taung Child. The injuries looked very similar to those seen in modern monkeys that have been attacked and killed by eagles.
Reference Simplification
This conclusion was reached by noting similarities in the damage to the skull and eye sockets of the Taung Child to the skulls of primates known to have been killed by modern eagles. The left hand side of the endocast of the Taung child is covered in beautiful calcium carbonate crystals.

Example 276 | Profile: lower_education_level | Language: English | ID/Index: 276
Source Text
Renal involvement can develop at any time and may be the only manifestation of SLE. It may be benign and asymptomatic or progressive and fatal.
Generated Simplification
Kidney problems can happen at any time in people with lupus (SLE). - Sometimes, these kidney issues don’t cause any symptoms and don’t lead to serious problems. - Other times, they can get worse over time and become life-threatening. If you have lupus, it’s important to talk to your doctor about your kidney health, even if you feel fine.
Reference Simplification

Example 278 | Profile: lower_education_level | Language: English | ID/Index: 278
Source Text
Vasoconstriction can change a lesion that does not limit blood flow into a severe or complete stenosis. Symptoms of unstable angina or myocardial infarction, ischemic stroke, or rest pain in the limbs may develop when unstable plaques rupture and acutely occlude a major artery, with superimposition of thrombosis or embolism.
Generated Simplification
When blood vessels tighten (a process called vasoconstriction), it can make a partially blocked area in the blood vessel much worse. This can lead to a severe or complete blockage of blood flow. If a fatty buildup in the blood vessel (called a plaque) becomes unstable and breaks open, it can cause serious problems. This is because the broken plaque can block a major blood vessel, especially if a blood clot forms on top of it or if pieces of the plaque travel to other areas. Here are some possible problems that can happen if this occurs: - **Unstable angina**: This is chest pain that happens suddenly and may not go away with rest. - **Heart attack (myocardial infarction)**: This happens when blood flow to the heart is completely blocked. - **Stroke (ischemic stroke)**: This occurs when blood flow to the brain is blocked. - **Rest pain in the limbs**: This is a severe pain in your arms or legs that happens even when you are not moving. If you experience any of these symptoms, it is very important to seek medical help right away. Talk to your doctor if you have concerns about your heart or blood vessels.
Reference Simplification
The first symptom of a narrowed artery may be pain or cramps at times when blood flow can not keep up with the tissues â need for oxygen.
BERTScore
Precision
0.022
Recall
0.235
F1
0.128

Example 279 | Profile: lower_education_level | Language: English | ID/Index: 279
Source Text
According to the fifth Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM), DID includes `` the presence of two or more distinct identities or personality states '' that alternate control of the individual 's behavior, accompanied by the inability to recall personal information beyond what is expected through normal forgetfulness.
Generated Simplification
Dissociative Identity Disorder (DID) is a mental health condition. - A person with DID has two or more different identities or personality states. - These identities take turns controlling the person’s behavior. - People with DID may also have trouble remembering personal information. This forgetfulness is more severe than normal memory lapses. If you think you or someone you know might have DID, it’s important to talk to a healthcare professional. They can help with diagnosis and treatment.
Reference Simplification
According to the DSM-IV, the symptoms of DID are `` the presence of two or more distinct identities or personality states '' (which are often called `` alters ''), as well as forgetting things that people normally do n't forget.

Example 298 | Profile: lower_education_level | Language: English | ID/Index: 298
Source Text
Arterial blood carries oxygen from inhaled air to all of the cells of the body, and venous blood carries carbon dioxide, a waste product of metabolism by cells, to the lungs to be exhaled.
Generated Simplification
Blood in your body has two main jobs: - **Arterial blood** (blood in your arteries) carries oxygen from the air you breathe to all the cells in your body. Your cells need oxygen to work properly. - **Venous blood** (blood in your veins) carries carbon dioxide, which is a waste your cells make when they use oxygen. This blood takes the carbon dioxide to your lungs, where you breathe it out. This process helps keep your body working and healthy.
Reference Simplification
Red blood cells carry oxygen and carbon dioxide around our body.

0.204

0.511

Example 299 | Profile: lower_education_level | Language: English | ID/Index: 299
Source Text
The kinetoplastids (also known as ` kinetoplastea ') are a group of single-cell flagellate protozoa, including a number of parasites responsible for serious diseases in humans and other animals, as well as various forms found in soil and aquatic environments.
Generated Simplification
Kinetoplastids are tiny, single-celled organisms that move using a tail-like structure called a flagellum. Some of these organisms can cause serious diseases in people and animals. However, not all kinetoplastids are harmful—some live in soil or water without causing any problems. If you have concerns about diseases caused by these organisms, talk to your doctor for more information.
Reference Simplification
The kinetoplastids are a group of single-cell flagellate Eukaryotes.

Item Question Score Reason
L2 2 The simplification explains 'flagellate' as 'move using a tail-like structure called a flagellum' in familiar language.

L4 2 The simplification avoids unnecessary jargon and uses simpler expressions like 'tiny, single-celled organisms' instead of 'single-cell flagellate protozoa.'
C2 2 The simplification provides a short background explanation of kinetoplastids, including their movement and habitats.
C3 2 The simplification makes the relationship between kinetoplastids and diseases explicit.
C4 1 The simplification uses a comparison ('tail-like structure') but does not provide additional examples or comparisons that could further aid understanding.
S1 2 The simplification splits the information into shorter, easier-to-process sentences.
S2 2 The simplification uses active voice, making the meaning clearer.
S3 2 Different ideas, such as harmful and non-harmful kinetoplastids, are clearly separated into distinct sentences.

P3 2 The simplification clearly states the next step of consulting a doctor if there are concerns.
P4 2 The simplification advises discussing concerns with a healthcare professional.
Overall checklist comment
The simplification is clear, patient-friendly, and effectively simplifies the source text while maintaining its meaning.
